task: I’m looking over a service agreement before I let anyone start, and I want to be sure I’m not overlooking anything that could matter later. What parts should I pay closest attention to, especially if the work turns out not to match what was promised?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — before you let work start, the parts that matter most are the ones that define **what was promised, what can change, and what happens if the work doesn’t match**.

Here’s the checklist I’d focus on:

### 1) Scope of work
- Exact services to be performed
- Materials, parts, and labor included
- Any exclusions or “not included” items
- Whether the agreement says the provider can decide the method or scope later

If the work later doesn’t match, this is the first place to compare against.

### 2) Price terms
- Total price or how it’s calculated
- Unit rates, hourly rates, minimum charges
- Taxes, fees, surcharges, trip charges, disposal fees, etc.
- Whether the price is fixed or estimated

### 3) Change-order / extra work terms
- Whether extra work must be approved **in writing**
- Who has authority to approve changes
- How price increases are supposed to be documented
- Whether they can bill for “necessary” work without getting approval first

This is one of the biggest issues if the final work doesn’t match the original promise.

### 4) Delivery / performance expectations
- Start and completion dates
- Deadlines or time windows
- Standards of quality or specifications
- Any warranties on labor or materials

### 5) Dispute / acceptance language
- What counts as acceptance of the work
- Whether silence or payment is treated as approval
- Any deadline to object to defects or billing problems
- Whether you waive objections by letting them proceed

### 6) Cancellation / termination terms
- Your right to cancel before work starts
- Fees for cancellation
- How notice must be given
- Whether unused deposits are refundable

### 7) Warranty and remedy terms
- What they promise if work is defective or incomplete
- Whether they will repair, replace, or refund
- Limits on your remedies
- Any disclaimer that weakens their promises

### 8) Signature and attachment details
- Make sure all exhibits, estimates, schedules, and addenda are attached
- Check that the signed version matches the version you reviewed
- Look for fine print that says attachments control or override the main agreement

### 9) Anything that gives them discretion
Watch for phrases like:
- “as needed”
- “at provider’s discretion”
- “subject to availability”
- “estimated only”
- “may be adjusted”
- “additional charges may apply”

Those can matter a lot later if the final work or bill changes.

### 10) Proof and documentation
Before work starts, try to confirm:
- The written estimate or order
- Any signed agreement
- Any price-change notice
- Any approval process for extra work
- Any cancellation or renewal terms

If you want, I can turn this into a **short pre-signing checklist** you can use line by line while reviewing the agreement.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the right list. Scope, price, change orders, and what counts as acceptance — those are the ones I’d watch first. If the paper says they can do “extra” work without my okay, that’s a problem. Same with vague wording about estimates or final price.

I’d also want the exact company name, license number, and a cancellation clause I can actually use without chasing somebody around. And I’d keep a copy of the signed version, not just their sales sheet.

So, yes — that covers what matters.
